claims.flip_evidence_text: except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account
claims.notes: Claim states an unqualified exclusion, but the contract subjects clause (i) to a disproportionate-impact carve-out.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any fact, circumstance, occurrence, effect, change, event or development that, individually or in the aggregate, has (a) resulted or would reasonably be expected to result in a material delay or impediment to the ability of the Company to consummate the Merger or the other Transactions, or (b) had or would reasonably be expected to have a material adverse effect on the assets, liabilities, business, financial condition or results of operations of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole; provided, however, that, in the case of clause (b), a Company Material Adverse Effect shall not be deemed to include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to (except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account): (i) conditions affecting the United States economy generally; (ii) political conditions (or changes in such conditions) in the United States (including the State of Delaware or any state in which the Company or the Company Subsidiaries operate), declared or undeclared acts of war, sabotage or terrorism, epidemics, pandemics or other contagion, including COVID-19 (including any escalation or general worsening of any of the foregoing) or national or international emergency in the United States or any other country or region of the world occurring after the date hereof; (iii) changes in the financial, credit, banking or securities markets in the United States or any other country or region in the world (including any disruption thereof and any decline in the price of any security or any market index) and including changes or developments in or relating to currency exchange or interest rates; (iv) changes required by GAAP (or interpretations thereof by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) or any Governmental Authority); (v) changes in any Laws (or interpretations thereof by a Governmental Authority); (vi) changes that are generally applicable to the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate; (vii) any failure by the Company to meet any internal or publicly available projections, forecasts or revenue or earnings predictions or any decline in the market price or trading volume of the capital stock of the Company (provided that the underlying causes of any such failure or decline may be considered in determining whether a Company Material Adverse Effect has occurred to the extent not otherwise excluded by another exception herein); (viii) the negotiation, execution or delivery of this Agreement, the performance by Company and the Company Subsidiaries of their obligations hereunder or the public announcement as to the identity of the parties hereto or pendency of the Merger or any of the other Transactions, including the impact of such public announcement on relationships, contractual or otherwise with customers, suppliers or employees of the Company and the Company Subsidiaries (it being understood that this clause (viii) shall not apply to any representation or warranty set forth in Section 4.5 (or the condition to Parent’s and Merger Sub’s obligation to commence the Migration Filing or consummate the Closing set forth in Section 7.2(a) or Section 7.5(a)), in each case solely to the extent related to the foregoing representations and warranties); (ix) changes in the Company’s credit rating (provided that the underlying causes of such decline may be considered in determining whether a Company Material Adverse Effect has occurred to the extent not otherwise excluded by another exception herein); (x) the occurrence of natural disasters or weather conditions adverse to the business being carried on by the Company and the Company Subsidiaries; (xi) stockholder litigation arising from or relating to this Agreement or the Merger, including any action alleging or asserting any misrepresentation or omission in any documents (including exhibits and all other information incorporated therein) filed with or furnished to the SEC; or (xii) any action taken or refrained from being taken by the Company that is required to be taken or prohibited from being taken, respectively pursuant to this Agreement, or is taken or refrained from being taken with the prior written consent or at the express direction of Parent. (Page 9)

Question:
FLS (MAE) applies to

Llama answer:
A Company Material Adverse Effect (MAE) does not include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to conditions affecting the United States economy generally.

Canonical claims:
- claim-272609852ea9: A Company Material Adverse Effect (MAE) does not include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to conditions affecting the United States economy generally.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt and the model answer (Llama answer).

Contract excerpt defines "Company Material Adverse Effect" and lists exceptions under clause (b). Specifically, clause (i) says: "conditions affecting the United States economy generally" are excluded (unless disproportionate).

Claim: "A Company Material Adverse Effect (MAE) does not include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to conditions affecting the United States economy generally."

This matches almost verbatim with the language in the contract excerpt: "a Company Material Adverse Effect shall not be deemed to include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to ... (i) conditions affecting the United States economy generally;"

Wait, there is a caveat: "except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company ... relative to other similarly situated companies ... in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account"

So the statement in the claim is not absolutely true because if the conditions disproportionately affect the company, the incremental disproportionate effect is taken into account. The claim says "does not include" which is a broad statement. However, the question is: "FLS (MAE) applies to" and the Llama answer is the claim.

I need to determine:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing=yes
- notes: optional

Correctness: The claim says MAE does not include [things] arising from or related to conditions affecting the US economy generally. This is mostly true but omits the disproportionality carve-out. Since the contract explicitly includes a disproportionality exception for clause (i), saying it "does not include" these things without qualification is an overstatement / incomplete. Therefore, it is partially_true.

Load bearing: Is this claim load-bearing for the answer? The question is "FLS (MAE) applies to". The Llama answer is essentially the claim. The claim is the core of the answer. So load_bearing_label = yes.

Flip evidence text: If load bearing = yes, I need to provide the text that would flip the claim. The flip would be the disproportionality carve-out. So something like: "except ... to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account"

Wait, the prompt says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should quote from the contract excerpt.

Let me re-read the exact wording in the excerpt: "provided, however, that, in the case of clause (b), a Company Material Adverse Effect shall not be deemed to include facts, circumstances, occurrences, effects, changes, events or developments arising from or related to (except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account): (i) conditions affecting the United States economy generally;"

So the flip evidence is the exception to the exception.

The claim is essentially a direct quote of the beginning of the exclusion but misses the parenthetical exception. It's partially true because it's true to the extent the disproportionality carve-out doesn't apply.

Load bearing: Yes, because the entire Llama answer is this claim. If this claim were false, the answer would be wrong.

Flip evidence text: The text that demonstrates the claim is not fully correct / that flips it. I should provide the exception text. Something concise: "except, in the case of clauses (i) ... to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account"

Actually, the prompt says "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This should be the text from the contract that shows the claim is not fully accurate (i.e., the text that "flips" the claim from true to false/partially true).

Let me think about whether it should be "partially_true" or "true". The claim is a direct quote of the contract, but omits the subsequent qualifier. Does the omission make it false? In legal annotation tasks like this, usually if a claim omits a material qualifier, it's considered partially true or false depending on severity. Here, the qualifier is that the exclusion does NOT apply if the effect is disproportionate. So saying it "does not include" is an unqualified statement that is false in the disproportionate case. Thus, partially_true is most accurate.

Wait, is there any nuance about "FLS (MAE) applies to"? FLS might mean "Forward Looking Statements"? No, the question is just "FLS (MAE) applies to". The Llama answer says MAE does not include conditions affecting the US economy generally. Maybe FLS stands for something else? The question is ambiguous, but I must evaluate the claim as given.

One more check: The contract says "shall not be deemed to include ... (i) conditions affecting the United States economy generally;". The claim says "does not include". This is synonymous. But the contract adds the disproportionality carve-out for clause (i). So the claim is incomplete.

I will label:
- correctness_label: partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: "except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account"
- notes: The claim ignores the disproportionate-impact carve-out for clause (i).

Wait, should the flip evidence include the clause (i) part? The flip evidence is the text that qualifies the claim. The claim is that it does not include these things. The flip is that it *does* include them to the extent they disproportionately affect the company. So the exception text is appropriate.

Let me make sure the output format is correct. The user wants for each claim:
- correctness_label
- load_bearing_label
- flip_evidence_text
- notes

So probably JSON or a structured list. The prompt doesn't specify exact JSON format, but usually these tasks want a JSON object or markdown. I'll provide a JSON-like structure or a clear bulleted list. Since the user said "For each claim return:", I should return fields for the specific claim ID.

I'll format as:

claim-272609852ea9:
- correctness_label: partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes
- flip_evidence_text: "except, in the case of clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi) or (x) below, to the extent disproportionately affecting the Company and the Company Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated companies in the industries in which the Company and the Company Subsidiaries operate, in which case only the incremental disproportionate effect shall be taken into account"
- notes: Claim omits the disproportionality carve-out applicable to clause (i).

Double check: Is the load_bearing_label definitely yes? The Llama answer *is* the claim. So yes, the claim is load-bearing.

If the Llama answer had multiple claims and this was just one of them, I might say no if it's not central. But here the answer consists solely of this claim. So definitely yes.

One final check: Is the claim "A Company Material Adverse Effect (MAE) does not include facts..." actually exactly what the contract says? Yes, the contract says "a Company Material Adverse Effect shall not be deemed to include facts...". The claim simplifies "shall not be deemed to include" to "does not include". That's fine. The issue is the missing parenthetical.

Could it be considered "true" because the exception is an exception to the exclusion (i.e., double negative)? Let's parse:
- Main definition: MAE = material adverse effect.
- Exclusion: MAE shall NOT be deemed to include [list of things like US economy].
- Exception to exclusion: [list including (i)] EXCEPT to the extent disproportionately affecting...

So the net effect is: MAE generally does NOT include US economy conditions, BUT it DOES include the incremental disproportionate effect.

So the claim "MAE does not include ... conditions affecting the US economy generally" is overbroad. Therefore partially_true.

Yes.

Let me write the final output clearly.
